author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "Therefore I say, He ordained that we should undergo death for the faith, which each man should do for the communion of the Church.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For Christianity makes progress by the deaths of its followers.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if death were feared by the faithful, no man would be found to live with perfect faith.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For the Lord Christ says, ‘Except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die, it abideth alone: but if it die, it bringeth forth much fruit.’"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: I should be glad to know which of your party it was who first threw himself over a precipice.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For truly that grain of corn was fruitful from which so great a crop of similar suicides has sprung.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Tell me, when you make mention of the words of the Lord, that He says a grain of wheat shall die and bring forth much fruit, why do you envy the real fruit, which has most truly sprung up throughout the whole world, and bring up against it all the charges of the tares or chaff which you have ever either heard of or invented?
